current_fact.fact_text: On April 29, 1483, the island of Gran Canaria—now a holiday destination—was fully conquered by the Crown of Castile after years of resistance by the Indigenous Guanche people. Beneath today’s resorts and beaches lies a layered history of colonization, cultural blending, and survival.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

current_fact.match_reason: An island’s conquest and complex identity fit an episode centered on another Mediterranean island, Sardegna, exploring how history and belonging shape island cultures.

queue.fact_text: On April 29, 1997, the Chemical Weapons Convention entered into force, creating a global treaty that bans the development, stockpiling, and use of chemical weapons. For the first time, an entire class of weapons of mass destruction was not just limited, but legally outlawed by much of the world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A landmark attempt to curtail inhumane warfare resonates with Afrobeat’s political legacy, especially Fela Kuti’s fierce critique of militarism and state violence.

queue.fact_text: On April 29, 2013, a natural gas leak triggered a powerful explosion in central Prague, blowing out windows and injuring 43 people. The blast served as a reminder that invisible infrastructure—pipes under streets, gas in walls—quietly carries both comfort and risk through our cities.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: A sudden, unintended explosion contrasts yet connects with an episode about accidents, imperfections, and how things that go wrong reshape how we see the built world.

queue.fact_text: Also on April 29, 2013, National Airlines Flight 102, a Boeing 747‑400 freighter, crashed shortly after takeoff from Bagram Airfield in Afghanistan when heavy cargo shifted backward, fatally destabilizing the aircraft. The accident underscored how even small miscalculations in load balance can overturn the might of one of aviation’s largest workhorses.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A crash caused by a small shift in weight aligns with an episode about sudden disruption, vulnerability, and the long, reflective work of recovery afterward.

queue.fact_text: On April 29, 2009 (commemorated annually), the international community marks the Day of Remembrance for all Victims of Chemical Warfare, tied closely to the Chemical Weapons Convention that came into force on this date in 1997. It’s a day that asks scientists, soldiers, and civilians alike to remember how invisible molecules can leave visible scars for generations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A remembrance day about banned weapons connects with an episode centered on combat sports and discipline, highlighting the contrast between regulated physical struggle and indiscriminate chemical harm.

queue.fact_text: Another thread of April 29 is cultural rather than explosive: across the 20th century, this date saw the growth of broadcast experiments like 3D TV and the globalization of sports like baseball—which in 2015 played to empty stands. Taken together, they show how technology keeps redefining what it means to gather, even when we’re physically apart.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: As TV and sports rethink how to reach people, a raw first radio episode mirrors that energy of inventing new ways to come together through sound and story.

queue.fact_text: If you zoom out from the battles and treaties of April 29, you see a pattern of borders crossed—Cook landing at Botany Bay, a U.S. carrier in the Suez Canal, armies stepping into Cambodia, and fleets clashing off Martinique. It’s a date when lines on maps have been redrawn again and again, often at great human cost.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: Stories of borders and crossings pair with an episode about people out of place—Sicilians in northern mountains—using music to navigate identity and landscape.

queue.fact_text: Seen from far above, April 29 on Earth is a patchwork of seismic shocks, storms, uprisings, and scientific discoveries—from the shaking basilica in 801 to a galaxy logged in 1826 and a spaceship launched in 1985. It’s a reminder that history doesn’t move in a straight line; it pulses like music, with quiet interludes and sudden crescendos.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: A reflective, planetary‑scale view of April 29 suits an episode built as a slow crescendo, moving through textures and regions like a sonic map of the day’s varied events.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1826, Scottish astronomer James Dunlop catalogued a strange, bright smudge in the southern sky that would later be known as Centaurus A—one of the closest active galaxies to Earth. Today we know it's a powerful radio galaxy with a supermassive black hole at its core, spewing jets of particles tens of thousands of light-years into space.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A galaxy discovery pairs naturally with an episode themed around 'space' and shifting perspective, echoing how astronomy turns emptiness into possibility.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1953, Los Angeles station KECA-TV broadcast one of the first experimental 3D television programs in the United States, featuring an episode of the sci‑fi series "Space Patrol." Viewers needed special polarized glasses, turning their living rooms into tiny cinemas where spaceships and ray guns seemed to float out of the screen.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

published.match_reason: An experimental 3D broadcast mirrors the exploratory, format-bending spirit of launching a new, immersive 'Evening Flows' format.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1985, Space Shuttle Challenger lifted off on mission STS‑51‑B, carrying Spacelab experiments ranging from materials science to the behavior of animals in microgravity. Inside, tiny crew members including monkeys and rats became some of the earliest non‑human frequent flyers of low Earth orbit.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: A shuttle flight packed with experiments and exploration fits a 'Destination Sedna' voyage through new territories in sound and space.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1986, the nuclear‑powered aircraft carrier USS Enterprise became the first ship of its kind to squeeze through the Suez Canal, threading an 1,100‑foot floating airbase through a narrow man‑made waterway. The transit highlighted how human engineering can merge ocean‑spanning technology with a ribbon of water carved through the desert.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A giant carrier slipping through a canal connects to a boat‑themed episode about navigating water, travel, and shared journeys at sea.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1770, James Cook’s ship Endeavour dropped anchor in a bay on Australia’s east coast that he named Botany Bay, inspired by the extraordinary variety of plants his botanists found there. The landing marked the beginning of a long thread of scientific exploration—and a painful chapter of colonization—for the continent’s First Peoples.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A coastal landing defined by curiosity about nature fits an episode about immersing in water and environment, moving fluidly between discovery and reflection.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 801, a powerful earthquake in the Central Apennines shook Rome and Spoleto, badly damaging the ancient basilica of San Paolo Fuori le Mura. Medieval chroniclers saw such quakes as signs from the heavens, long before plate tectonics revealed the restless geology beneath Italy’s mountains.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, destructive earthquake is like the Earth's own electric shock, matching the raw, jolting energy of this high‑voltage rock selection.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1991, the Racha earthquake struck northern Georgia with a magnitude of 7.0, unleashing landslides that wiped out entire villages. The event pushed scientists to study how mountain building and seismic risk are tightly intertwined in the Caucasus.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: In the wake of a major quake, societies must slowly rebuild and adapt, resonating with an episode about movement, perspective shifts, and finding new rhythms after disruption.

published.fact_text: Also on April 29, 1991, a colossal tropical cyclone slammed into Bangladesh near Chittagong with winds near 155 mph, driving a wall of water over the low‑lying coast. Despite years of experience with storms, more than 138,000 people died, highlighting both the vulnerability of densely populated deltas and the life‑saving power of early‑warning systems that would improve afterward.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A devastating cyclone and the communities rebuilding afterward speak directly to an episode about storms, seasons, and the belief that rain—and hardship—do not last forever.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 2015, a Major League Baseball game between the Baltimore Orioles and Chicago White Sox was played in a completely empty stadium—officially closed to fans due to civil unrest in Baltimore. The crack of the bat and calls from the dugout echoed eerily around tens of thousands of vacant seats, turning a communal sport into something strangely intimate and surreal.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: A silent stadium focuses attention on the pure sound of the game, mirroring an episode that strips away noise and algorithms to highlight intentional, present‑moment listening.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1975, near the end of the Vietnam War, North Vietnamese forces completed their capture of South Vietnam–held outposts in the Trường Sa (Spratly) Islands. These remote specks of land, barely visible on most maps, would later sit at the heart of complex disputes over sea routes, fisheries, and undersea resources.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Island outposts contested in war echo themes of territory, resistance, and survival that run through Tuareg music about land and displacement.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1970, U.S. and South Vietnamese forces launched the Cambodian Campaign, crossing the border in an effort to sever the Ho Chi Minh Trail supply routes. The operation extended the Vietnam War into yet another country, triggering massive protests at home and reshaping public opinion about the conflict.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A controversial war escalation that fueled protest movements matches an episode centered on Rage Against the Machine, whose music embodies anti‑war and anti‑imperialist resistance.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1974, President Richard Nixon announced he would release edited transcripts of secret White House tapes related to the Watergate scandal. The pages revealed profanity, paranoia, and political scheming, and the obvious gaps only deepened the public’s appetite for the unfiltered truth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A scandal built on recorded conversations and public distrust parallels an episode unpacking our complex relationship with technology, information, and what’s really being recorded.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1992, after four police officers were acquitted in the beating of Rodney King, Los Angeles erupted into days of unrest that left 63 people dead and thousands injured. The uprising forced the United States to confront—once again—deep fault lines of racism, policing, and inequality that still reverberate through culture and music.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: The LA riots and Rodney King case connect strongly with modern conscious hip‑hop’s focus on systemic racism and identity, themes explored through Kendrick Lamar in this episode.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1916, leaders of the Easter Rising in Dublin surrendered after six days of urban warfare against British forces. Although the rebellion was militarily crushed, the execution of its leaders transformed public opinion in Ireland and turned a failed insurrection into a powerful symbol of independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A failed uprising that becomes a symbol of resistance matches an episode about Residente, whose politically charged music turns personal and collective struggle into lasting cultural impact.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1862, Union forces under General Henry Halleck began the Siege of Corinth, a slow, methodical encirclement of a key Confederate rail junction in Mississippi. Instead of a single dramatic battle, the Union advanced by building miles of trenches, inching forward each day in a kind of grim, muddy choreography.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A drawn‑out siege won through small, incremental advances mirrors an episode about learning as a slow, persistent process where progress is built trench by trench.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1781, British and French fleets clashed off Martinique in the Battle of Fort Royal, a naval duel fought largely out of sight of land. Amid smoke and shifting winds, both sides maneuvered huge wooden ships armed with dozens of cannons, testing tactics that would define sea power for decades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A historic sea battle off an island pairs with a boat‑themed, coastal‑sound journey—two very different kinds of voyages moving across storied waters.

published.fact_text: On April 29, 1483, the island of Gran Canaria—now a holiday destination—was fully conquered by the Crown of Castile after years of resistance by the Indigenous Guanche people. Beneath today’s resorts and beaches lies a layered history of colonization, cultural blending, and survival.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
